# SSH for Bioinformaticians: Config, Keys, and the Things That Actually Break

Every chapter in Part VI assumes one quietly heroic thing: that you can type `ssh
user@cluster` and end up at a usable shell on the other side. The HPC user
documentation at every institution starts the same way — a short paragraph titled
*Connecting to the Cluster* with one example command and a cheerful invitation to
"contact us if you have problems." In practice, the gap between that documentation and
a working connection is where new HPC users spend their first miserable week. The login
node demands a key the new user has not generated. The institution requires Duo
two-factor authentication that the docs forgot to mention. The compute node is on a
private network behind a bastion host that nobody told you about. The key was generated
on the laptop but never copied to the cluster. The key was copied but the file
permissions are `0644` and `sshd` is silently refusing it. The connection works from
the office but not from a hotel where outbound port 22 is blocked. VS Code Remote-SSH
fails with an opaque error about an "extension host" that has no obvious analog in any
standard SSH manual.

This chapter is about closing that gap. SSH is the single most important piece of
infrastructure in a working bioinformatician's day — it is the connection over which
you submit jobs, edit code, transfer terabytes, tunnel Jupyter sessions, and run
agentic AI tools. When SSH is working you do not think about it. When it is not, no
other work happens. The good news is that SSH is well-documented, predictable, and
once configured rarely needs to be touched again. The trick is the configuration.

A working `~/.ssh/config` is the difference between typing
`ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_hpc -p 2022 -J jump.cluster.edu yzhang@compute-01.private.cluster.edu`
fifty times a day and typing `ssh hpc`. We will get you to the second one.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

1. Generate an Ed25519 SSH key pair, set the correct file permissions, and explain
   what the public and private halves do.
2. Install your public key on a remote host, both with `ssh-copy-id` and by editing
   `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` directly.
3. Write a `~/.ssh/config` file with `Host` aliases, `IdentityFile` entries, and
   per-host options so that day-to-day commands are short and consistent.
4. Use `ssh-agent`, `ssh-add`, and the `AddKeysToAgent` directive to enter your
   passphrase once per session instead of once per connection.
5. Connect through bastion / jump hosts using `ProxyJump`, and re-use a single SSH
   connection across many sessions with `ControlMaster`.
6. Transfer files reliably using `rsync` over SSH, including resumable transfers for
   multi-terabyte sequencing datasets.
7. Set up a port-forwarding tunnel to access a Jupyter or RStudio Server running on
   an HPC compute node from your laptop browser.
8. Diagnose the most common SSH error messages — `Permission denied (publickey)`,
   `Host key verification failed`, "permissions are too open" — and fix them.

## How SSH Actually Works

SSH (Secure Shell) is a protocol for running a shell, transferring files, or forwarding
network traffic over an encrypted connection between a **client** (your laptop) and a
**server** (the HPC login node). You do not need to understand the cryptography in
detail to use SSH well, but two ideas earn their keep.

**Public-key cryptography** is a system in which a key pair consists of two
mathematically linked files: a *private key* that you keep secret and never share, and
a *public key* that you can hand to anyone. Anything encrypted with the public key can
be decrypted only with the private key, and a signature made with the private key can
be verified using only the public key. SSH uses this so the server can prove you hold
the private key without you ever sending it across the network.

**Host keys** are the server's identity. The first time you connect to a server, your
client receives the server's public host key and asks you to confirm a fingerprint.
Once you accept, that fingerprint is stored in `~/.ssh/known_hosts`. On every future
connection, the client verifies that the server still presents the same key. If the key
changes — perhaps because the cluster was rebuilt, or perhaps because someone is
attempting a man-in-the-middle attack — SSH refuses to connect until you investigate.

The two key concepts together give you the table below.

| Key | Lives on | Used to prove |
|---|---|---|
| Your **private key** (`~/.ssh/id_ed25519`) | Your laptop only | *You* are who you say you are |
| Your **public key** (`~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`) | Pasted into the server's `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` | The server can verify *you* |
| Server **host key** (`/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key`) | The HPC server only | The server can prove *its* identity to you |
| Server **host public key** (your `~/.ssh/known_hosts`) | Your laptop, after first connection | You can verify the *server* on every reconnect |

A typical login goes:

```
[your laptop]                              [hpc.cluster.edu]
     |                                            |
     |  TCP connect to port 22  ───────────────►  |
     |  ◄──── server presents host public key  ── |
     |  (laptop checks against known_hosts)       |
     |                                            |
     |  ──── "I am yzhang"  ───────────────────►  |
     |  ◄──── challenge for yzhang's key  ──────  |
     |  (laptop signs with private key)           |
     |  ──── signature  ───────────────────────►  |
     |                              (sshd checks  |
     |                               authorized_  |
     |                               keys)        |
     |  ◄──── encrypted shell session  ─────────  |
```

Everything else in this chapter — agents, jump hosts, tunnels, multiplexing — is built
on top of this same handshake.

## Generating Your Key Pair

In 2026, the right key type to generate is **Ed25519**. It is faster, smaller, and based
on a more modern elliptic-curve construction than the older RSA keys that still appear
in many institutional documentation pages. RSA is still supported by every SSH server,
but a 4096-bit RSA key is roughly ten times larger than an Ed25519 key and offers no
practical security advantage. Use Ed25519 unless you are forced into RSA by an ancient
appliance.

Generate a new key with `ssh-keygen`:

```bash
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "yzhang@tufts.edu Tufts HPC 2026-04"
```

The `-C` flag adds a comment that appears at the end of the public key. Use it to record
*who* the key belongs to and *when* it was generated — this is invaluable when you have
six keys for six clusters and need to identify which one is which two years later.

`ssh-keygen` will prompt you for a file location and a passphrase:

```text
Enter file in which to save the key (/home/yzhang/.ssh/id_ed25519):
Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):
Enter same passphrase again:
```

**Always set a passphrase.** A private key without a passphrase is a plain-text password
sitting on your laptop's disk. If your laptop is stolen or compromised, an empty
passphrase means the attacker has immediate access to every server where your key is
installed. With a passphrase, the private key is encrypted at rest; the attacker has to
crack it before the key is useful. We will use `ssh-agent` shortly so you only type the
passphrase once per session, not once per connection.

After `ssh-keygen` runs, you have two files:

| File | Permission | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` | `600` (read/write for owner only) | Private key — never share |
| `~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub` | `644` (readable by all) | Public key — paste anywhere |

The `~/.ssh` directory itself must be `700` (only your user can enter it). On a fresh
account, this is set automatically by `ssh-keygen`, but if you ever copy your `~/.ssh`
directory between machines or unzip a backup, the permissions can come out wrong. SSH
*refuses to use* a private key whose permissions are too permissive, and the error
message is one of the most common entries in any HPC help desk's queue:

```text
@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
@         WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE!          @
@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
Permissions 0644 for '/home/yzhang/.ssh/id_ed25519' are
too open.
It is required that your private key files are NOT
accessible by others.
```

The fix is one line:

```bash
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
chmod 644 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config           # if you have one
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys  # on any server
chmod 644 ~/.ssh/known_hosts
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## The Private Key Never Leaves Your Laptop

There is exactly one operation in the SSH workflow that involves the private key
file: your local SSH client reads it (or the agent reads it once) and uses it to sign
challenges. The private key is *never* sent over the network, *never* uploaded to a
server, and *never* embedded in a Git repository. If a documentation page asks you to
"upload your private key" anywhere, that documentation is wrong. The only file that
goes on remote servers is the **public** half (`id_ed25519.pub`).
:::

## Installing Your Key on a Remote Host

For the server to recognize your key, it must contain your public key in
`~/.ssh/authorized_keys` for the account you are logging in as. The standard tool for
installing a key is `ssh-copy-id`:

```bash
ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub yzhang@hpc.cluster.edu
```

`ssh-copy-id` will prompt for your password (this is the one-and-only time you should
need it) and then append your public key to the remote `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`,
creating the file with correct permissions if it does not exist. Test by reconnecting:
the server should authenticate using your key without asking for a password.

When `ssh-copy-id` Is Not Available

Some clusters, especially those that require Duo or some other two-factor on every
password login, will not let `ssh-copy-id` work cleanly. Other environments — Windows
without a Unix shell, restricted appliances, or shared accounts where you do not have
write access via SSH — require manual installation. The procedure is short:

```bash
# 1. On your laptop, copy the public key to your clipboard or screen
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

# 2. SSH to the cluster (using your password, or a web terminal, or whatever
#    you currently have)
ssh yzhang@hpc.cluster.edu

# 3. On the cluster, set up the directory and append the key
mkdir -p ~/.ssh
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
echo "ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3Nza... yzhang@tufts.edu" >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
```

The `authorized_keys` file is a plain text file with **one public key per line**. Each
line has three whitespace-separated fields: the key type (`ssh-ed25519`), the base64
key data, and the comment. There can be many lines — one per device or institution —
and you can edit the file with any text editor. When the server authenticates a login,
it tries every key in the file until one matches.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pro-Tip: Use One Key Per Laptop, Not One Key Per Cluster

A common new-user mistake is to generate a fresh key on every cluster they join,
ending up with `id_ed25519_tufts`, `id_ed25519_xsede`, `id_ed25519_aws`, and a tangle
of `IdentityFile` entries. The cleaner pattern is one key per *device* (your laptop,
your desktop, your phone) installed on every cluster you use. When you lose a laptop
or leave an institution, you revoke or remove that one key from every
`authorized_keys` file. The unit of trust is the device that holds the private key,
not the cluster the key talks to.
:::

## Per-Host Configuration with `~/.ssh/config`

`~/.ssh/config` is the single most under-taught skill in HPC training. It lets you
define short aliases, default options, and per-host overrides for every cluster you
use, so that day-to-day commands shrink from a paragraph to a word.

The file is read top-to-bottom by the SSH client every time you run `ssh`, `scp`,
`sftp`, or `rsync -e ssh`. Each block starts with `Host <pattern>` and lists indented
options that apply when the pattern matches the destination you typed.

A real-world block for a Tufts HPC user might look like this:

```sshconfig
# ── Tufts HPC ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Host hpc
    HostName              login.pax.tufts.edu
    User                  yzhang
    Port                  22
    IdentityFile          ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
    IdentitiesOnly        yes
    ForwardAgent          no
    ServerAliveInterval   60
    ServerAliveCountMax   3
    ControlMaster         auto
    ControlPath           ~/.ssh/cm/%r@%h:%p
    ControlPersist        10m
```

With this in place, all of these commands now work and refer to the same login node:

```bash
ssh hpc
scp counts.tsv hpc:/scratch/yzhang/rnaseq/
rsync -av results/ hpc:/scratch/yzhang/rnaseq/results/
sftp hpc
```

What each option does:

| Option | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `HostName` | The actual DNS name to connect to. The `Host` line is your alias; `HostName` is the truth. |
| `User` | Remote username. Without this, SSH uses your local username. |
| `Port` | TCP port. Defaults to 22. Some sites use a non-standard port (often 2022) to reduce log noise from internet scanners. |
| `IdentityFile` | Which private key to offer. You can repeat this option to try several. |
| `IdentitiesOnly yes` | Offer **only** the keys listed in this block, not every key your agent knows about. Prevents "too many authentication failures" errors when you have many keys. |
| `ForwardAgent` | Whether to forward your agent to the remote host. Default `no`; see the warning later. |
| `ServerAliveInterval` | Seconds between keep-alive packets from the client to the server. `60` keeps NAT routers from dropping idle connections. |
| `ServerAliveCountMax` | How many missed keep-alives before the client gives up. With `Interval 60` and `CountMax 3`, the client disconnects after 3 minutes of silence. |
| `ControlMaster` / `ControlPath` / `ControlPersist` | Connection multiplexing. Discussed in detail below. |

You can also use wildcards. The block below applies sensible defaults to *every*
host, and the more specific blocks above can override individual options:

```sshconfig
# ── Defaults for every host ─────────────────────────────────────────────
Host *
    AddKeysToAgent       yes
    UseKeychain          yes        # macOS only; integrates with Keychain
    ServerAliveInterval  60
    HashKnownHosts       no         # set to yes for stricter privacy
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Order Matters in `ssh_config`

When several blocks match a host, SSH uses the *first* value it sees for each
option. A common mistake is putting `Host *` defaults at the top and then
overriding them in a more specific block — the override is ignored. Either put
specific blocks first and the wildcard last, or restructure so each option
appears only once on the path that matches.
:::

A more complete `~/.ssh/config` for a working bioinformatician might look like:

```sshconfig
# ── Defaults ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Host *
    AddKeysToAgent      yes
    UseKeychain         yes
    ServerAliveInterval 60
    ControlMaster       auto
    ControlPath         ~/.ssh/cm/%r@%h:%p
    ControlPersist      10m

# ── Tufts HPC ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Host hpc
    HostName     login.pax.tufts.edu
    User         yzhang
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# ── A compute node behind the login node ───────────────────────────────
# Reach an interior node only routable from the login host.
Host hpc-compute-*
    User       yzhang
    ProxyJump  hpc
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# ── A collaborator's institution ───────────────────────────────────────
Host bigsky
    HostName   login01.bigsky.example.edu
    User       y_zhang
    Port       2022
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# ── Personal cloud VM ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
Host devbox
    HostName   18.222.13.45
    User       ubuntu
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_aws
    IdentitiesOnly yes
```

Before this file existed, every interaction with a different cluster required a long
command line and a moment of remembering the right username, port, and key. After it
exists, every command is one short word.

## `ssh-agent` and Key Forwarding

You set a passphrase on your private key. You should not have to type it every time
you run `ssh hpc`. The mechanism that solves this is **ssh-agent**, a small background
program that holds decrypted private keys in memory for the duration of your login
session.

The workflow:

```bash
# Start the agent (most desktop OSes do this for you on login)
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"

# Add a key to the agent — this prompts for the passphrase once
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

# Now every ssh/scp/rsync command uses the loaded key without prompting
ssh hpc
```

If you have `AddKeysToAgent yes` in your `~/.ssh/config`, the agent is updated
automatically on first use of a key — you do not need to remember to run `ssh-add`.

On macOS, `UseKeychain yes` causes the OS to remember the passphrase across reboots in
the system Keychain, so you type it exactly once, ever. On Linux desktops, `gnome-
keyring` and `kwallet` provide similar integration. On Windows, the OpenSSH agent
service serves the same purpose; enable it once and it persists.

To see what keys are loaded:

```bash
ssh-add -l                 # list fingerprints of loaded keys
ssh-add -L                 # list full public keys
ssh-add -D                 # remove all keys from the agent
ssh-add -t 28800 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519   # add for 8 hours, then auto-expire
```

### Agent Forwarding (and Why It's a Liability)

`ForwardAgent yes` (or the `-A` flag) tells SSH to forward your local agent to the
remote host. This means a process on the *remote* host can talk to your *local* agent
and ask it to sign things — for example, to make further SSH connections to a third
host using your local keys, without needing the keys themselves to live on the remote.

This is genuinely useful. The most common case in bioinformatics is `git pull` from
GitHub on a cluster: with agent forwarding, the cluster can authenticate to GitHub
using your local SSH key without the key ever being copied to the cluster.

It is also a security exposure. Anyone with root access on the remote host — or any
attacker who has compromised it — can use the forwarded agent socket to authenticate
as you to anything else your agent can talk to. On a shared HPC login node, that
"anyone with root" includes the system administrators by definition.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Be Selective About Agent Forwarding

Default `ForwardAgent` to `no` and enable it only on hosts you trust. The `-A`
command-line flag is a useful one-off escape hatch when you really do need to push
to GitHub from the cluster. Even better is `ForwardAgent yes` only for specific
known-good hosts, or use `IdentityAgent` plus a per-host agent socket so you can
limit which keys are exposed.
:::

A safer pattern for the GitHub case is to install a *deploy key* on the cluster
specifically for the project, with no passphrase but limited to read-only access on
one repository. This avoids forwarding entirely.

## Jump Hosts and `ProxyJump`

Many HPC environments do not let you SSH directly to compute or interior service
nodes. Instead, you must first SSH to a public-facing **bastion** or **login node**,
and from there onward to your intended target. The classical way to do this is to
manually run two commands:

```bash
ssh hpc                  # land on the login node
ssh compute-01           # then jump from login to compute
```

The modern way, available since OpenSSH 7.3, is **ProxyJump**:

```bash
ssh -J yzhang@login.pax.tufts.edu compute-01.private.cluster.edu
```

`-J` tells your local SSH client to first connect to the jump host and then tunnel a
second SSH connection through it to the final destination. The end result is a single
shell on `compute-01`. Authentication happens *separately* against each hop, using
your local keys; the jump host never sees your private key, only forwards encrypted
traffic.

Of course, you do not want to type that long command. Put it in `~/.ssh/config`:

```sshconfig
Host hpc
    HostName     login.pax.tufts.edu
    User         yzhang
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

Host compute-*
    User         yzhang
    ProxyJump    hpc
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
```

Now `ssh compute-01` does the right thing automatically, including the jump through
`hpc`. `scp` and `rsync` work too:

```bash
rsync -av results/ compute-01:/scratch/yzhang/results/
```

::: {.callout-note}
## `ProxyJump` Replaces `ProxyCommand`

Older tutorials show a `ProxyCommand ssh -W %h:%p hpc` recipe to achieve the same
effect. `ProxyJump` is a cleaner, well-tested replacement and is the recommended
form on any OpenSSH 7.3 (2016) or newer. The `ProxyCommand` form is still useful
when you need to pipe through a non-SSH tool, like `cloudflared` for a Cloudflare
Tunnel — but for SSH-to-SSH hops, prefer `ProxyJump`.
:::

You can chain jumps for very locked-down networks:

```bash
ssh -J jump1,jump2 final-target
```

or in config:

```sshconfig
Host final-target
    ProxyJump jump1,jump2
```

## Connection Multiplexing

Every new SSH connection has a startup cost: TCP handshake, key exchange, authentication
(possibly with two-factor), shell setup. On a high-latency network or a 2FA-protected
cluster, this can be three to ten seconds *per connection*. A loop that runs `ssh hpc
"squeue -u yzhang"` once a minute, or an `rsync` that opens dozens of parallel SSH
connections under the hood, pays this cost over and over.

**Connection multiplexing** lets the client open one master connection and route many
subsequent sessions through it. Subsequent sessions skip the handshake entirely —
they are functionally instant.

Add this to a `Host` block (or to `Host *` for everywhere):

```sshconfig
ControlMaster   auto
ControlPath     ~/.ssh/cm/%r@%h:%p
ControlPersist  10m
```

What each line does:

- **`ControlMaster auto`**: if no master connection exists yet for this destination,
  create one; otherwise reuse it.
- **`ControlPath`**: where to put the Unix-domain socket that subsequent sessions use
  to attach to the master. The `%r@%h:%p` template gives a unique socket per
  user/host/port combination.
- **`ControlPersist 10m`**: keep the master alive for 10 minutes after the last
  session disconnects. Lets a quick subsequent reconnect avoid the full handshake.

You need to create the directory once:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.ssh/cm
chmod 700 ~/.ssh/cm
```

Test the difference. With multiplexing off:

```bash
time ssh hpc echo hi      # ~3 seconds
time ssh hpc echo hi      # ~3 seconds again
```

With multiplexing on, after the first connection sets up the master:

```bash
time ssh hpc echo hi      # ~3 seconds (sets up master)
time ssh hpc echo hi      # ~0.05 seconds (reuses master)
```

For a `rsync`, `scp`, or scripted loop that opens hundreds of short SSH sessions, the
speedup is dramatic.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Multiplexing Is a Footgun on Shared Workstations

The control socket is a Unix file in your home directory. Anyone who can read your
home directory and connect to that socket can reuse your authenticated SSH
connection without re-authenticating. On a shared lab machine where other accounts
have read access to your homedir (rare but it happens), turn multiplexing off, or
put the `ControlPath` somewhere only you can reach. On your own laptop, this is not
a meaningful concern.
:::

You can manage the master connection explicitly:

```bash
ssh -O check hpc           # is the master alive?
ssh -O exit hpc            # tear down the master immediately
```

Tearing down is occasionally necessary if a network change has left the master in a
half-broken state and new connections start hanging.

## File Transfer Over SSH

SSH carries more than interactive shells. Three closely related tools — `scp`, `sftp`,
and `rsync` — handle file transfer, all over the same SSH connection.

### `scp`: Simple, Now Slightly Deprecated

`scp` was for many years the default tool for one-off file copies:

```bash
scp counts.tsv hpc:/scratch/yzhang/rnaseq/
scp -r results/ hpc:/scratch/yzhang/rnaseq/results/
scp hpc:/scratch/yzhang/results/de_results.tsv ./
```

Beginning with OpenSSH 9.0 (April 2022), `scp` switched its default transport from
the legacy SCP protocol to SFTP under the hood. The user-facing command is the same,
but the change fixed a long-standing class of subtle bugs around filenames containing
shell metacharacters. The OpenSSH project now considers the SCP protocol "outdated,
inflexible, and not readily fixed" and recommends migrating to `sftp` or `rsync` for
new work.

For a single small file with a simple name, `scp` is fine. For anything else, prefer
`rsync` (for sync semantics) or `sftp` (for interactive transfer).

### `sftp`: Interactive Transfer

`sftp` opens an SSH session that exposes a small FTP-like interface for browsing
remote directories and copying files:

```bash
sftp hpc
sftp> cd /scratch/yzhang/rnaseq
sftp> ls
sftp> get results.tsv          # download
sftp> put samples.csv          # upload
sftp> mput *.bam               # upload many
sftp> bye
```

It is useful when you do not know exactly which files you want, or when you want to
poke around interactively without keeping a shell open.

### `rsync`: The Workhorse

For any non-trivial transfer — multiple files, large directories, anything you might
need to resume — `rsync` is the right tool. `rsync` only transfers what has changed,
preserves permissions and timestamps, can resume interrupted transfers, and over SSH
gets all of SSH's encryption and authentication for free.

The everyday invocation:

```bash
rsync -av --partial --progress -e ssh \
    /local/path/ hpc:/scratch/yzhang/path/
```

| Flag | Effect |
|---|---|
| `-a` | Archive mode: recursive + preserves permissions, timestamps, symlinks, etc. |
| `-v` | Verbose: show files being transferred. |
| `--partial` | Keep partially-transferred files on interruption so a re-run resumes. |
| `--progress` | Show per-file progress (use `-P` as a shortcut for `--partial --progress`). |
| `-e ssh` | Use SSH as the transport. Default since rsync 2.6.0; explicit `-e ssh` lets you pass extra ssh flags, e.g., `-e "ssh -i ~/.ssh/special_key"`. |
| `-z` | Compress in transit. Useful for slow networks; usually not worth it on fast intra-campus links. |
| `--checksum` | Compare files by checksum instead of size+mtime. Slow but rigorous; useful when you suspect corruption. |
| `--delete` | Delete files on the destination that no longer exist on the source. Essential for true mirroring; dangerous if you ever get the direction backwards. |

::: {.callout-warning}
## Trailing Slashes Decide What Gets Copied

`rsync -av /data/ hpc:/scratch/data/` copies *the contents of* `/data/` into
`/scratch/data/`. `rsync -av /data hpc:/scratch/data/` copies *the directory
itself*, creating `/scratch/data/data/`. The trailing `/` on the *source* is the
difference. This bites everyone at least once. Do a `--dry-run` first:

```bash
rsync -av --dry-run --partial --progress \
    /local/path/ hpc:/scratch/yzhang/path/
```
:::

For selective transfer, `--include` and `--exclude` filter what `rsync` considers:

```bash
# Transfer only fastq files from a deeply nested directory
rsync -av --partial --progress \
    --include='*/' --include='*.fastq.gz' --exclude='*' \
    /local/seq_run/ hpc:/scratch/yzhang/run01/

# Transfer everything except large intermediate BAM files
rsync -av --partial --progress \
    --exclude='*.bam' --exclude='*.bam.bai' \
    /local/results/ hpc:/scratch/yzhang/results/
```

For the largest transfers — terabyte-scale, cross-institution, intermittent network —
`rsync` will work but Globus is usually better; see Chapter 13's *Data Transfer*
section.

## Tunnels: Port Forwarding for Jupyter, RStudio, and Friends

A common HPC scenario: you have started a Jupyter Lab server on a compute node so it
has access to the cluster's CPU, memory, and GPU resources. Jupyter is listening on
port 8888 of the compute node, but the compute node is on a private network and has
no route from your laptop. You want to open `http://localhost:8888` in your laptop
browser and have it land on that Jupyter server.

The mechanism is **local port forwarding**, expressed with the `-L` flag:

```
ssh -L LOCAL_PORT:REMOTE_HOST:REMOTE_PORT user@gateway
```

This tells the SSH client to listen on `LOCAL_PORT` on your laptop. Anything that
connects to that local port is tunneled through the SSH connection to `gateway`, and
from `gateway` is forwarded to `REMOTE_HOST:REMOTE_PORT`.

A complete worked example for a Jupyter Lab session on an HPC compute node:

```bash
# ── Step 1: On the cluster, request an interactive job and start Jupyter ──
ssh hpc
srun --pty -p batch -c 4 --mem=16G -t 4:00:00 /bin/bash
# (now on a compute node, e.g., compute-042)

module load python/3.12
jupyter lab --no-browser --ip=0.0.0.0 --port=8888
# Note the line that says: http://compute-042:8888/?token=abc123...
```

Jupyter prints a URL with a token. Keep that terminal open and copy the token.

```bash
# ── Step 2: On your laptop, open the tunnel through the login node ──────
ssh -N -L 8888:compute-042:8888 hpc
```

The flags:

- `-N` says "do not run a remote command" — we only want the tunnel, not a shell.
- `-L 8888:compute-042:8888` listens on local port 8888, forwards through `hpc`, and
  connects from `hpc` to `compute-042:8888`. Note that `compute-042` is resolved on
  the *cluster's* internal DNS, not your laptop's.

Leave that terminal alone — closing it kills the tunnel.

```bash
# ── Step 3: Open in your laptop browser ─────────────────────────────────
# http://localhost:8888/?token=abc123...
```

Your browser connects to `localhost:8888`, the SSH tunnel forwards through `hpc` to
`compute-042:8888`, and Jupyter responds. The browser thinks it is talking to a local
service; the kernel actually has 4 cores and 16 GB on the cluster.

This pattern works for RStudio Server (port 8787), TensorBoard (6006), MLflow (5000),
PyMOL remote, IGV, or anything else that listens on a TCP port.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pro-Tip: Bake Common Tunnels Into `~/.ssh/config`

If you launch the same tunnel daily, define it in config and skip the long
command:

```sshconfig
Host hpc-jupyter
    HostName     login.pax.tufts.edu
    User         yzhang
    IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
    LocalForward 8888 compute-042:8888
    LocalForward 8787 compute-042:8787
```

Then `ssh hpc-jupyter` opens both forwards. Combine with `-N` if you only want the
tunnel and no shell.
:::

### Remote Forwarding (`-R`): The Other Direction

Less common, but worth knowing about: `-R` does the reverse. It listens on a port on
the *remote* host and forwards traffic back to your laptop. The classic use case is
exposing a development service running on your laptop to a teammate on a shared
server, or punching a return path through a NAT for testing webhooks. In day-to-day
bioinformatics, `-L` is the one you will use; `-R` exists, mostly for completeness.

## VS Code Remote-SSH and Cursor

By 2026, the dominant interactive HPC editing experience is **VS Code Remote-SSH** (or
**Cursor**, which is a fork of VS Code with the same Remote-SSH machinery). Instead of
editing files locally and re-uploading, or struggling with `vim` over a slow link, you
open a window of VS Code on your laptop that runs a small server on the cluster and
edits files in place at near-local speed, with full IDE features.

Both tools read your `~/.ssh/config`. Once `ssh hpc` works from a terminal, opening
`hpc` from VS Code's "Connect to Host" command also works, because it is the same
config file. This is one of the cleanest reasons to invest in your SSH config: every
SSH-aware tool inherits it for free.

The first time you connect, VS Code installs its server-side component into
`~/.vscode-server/` (Cursor uses `~/.cursor-server/`). On subsequent connections, only
the changed extensions are updated.

::: {.callout-warning}
## VS Code Server Will Eat Your Home Quota

`~/.vscode-server` and `~/.cursor-server` quietly accumulate hundreds of megabytes
to several gigabytes of binaries, extensions, and caches. On HPC clusters where
home directories have small quotas (5–50 GB) and contain conda environments and
project scripts, this is enough to silently push you over quota and break unrelated
jobs. Two mitigations:

- Periodically clean up old server installs:
  `rm -rf ~/.vscode-server/data/CachedExtensionVSIXs ~/.vscode-server/extensions/*-old`
- Symlink the server directory to scratch:
  `mv ~/.vscode-server /scratch/$USER/.vscode-server &&`
  `ln -s /scratch/$USER/.vscode-server ~/.vscode-server`
  (note: only if scratch is *not* purged aggressively, or you accept the cost of
  re-installing on purge).
:::

The other VS Code Remote pitfall worth mentioning: an extension that runs a heavy
process (a language server, an AI coding assistant doing project-wide indexing) runs
that process on the **server side**, which on an HPC cluster means the **login
node**. This is exactly the "do not run computation on the login node" violation that
Chapter 13 warns against. For heavy work, attach VS Code to an interactive Slurm
session on a compute node instead, e.g., by SSH-ing into the node directly via
`ProxyJump`.

## Two-Factor Authentication on HPC

Most universities and many research institutions now require two-factor
authentication (2FA) — Duo, Microsoft Authenticator, Yubikey, or institution-issued
TOTP — on every interactive login. From the SSH perspective, 2FA is added by an
extra `pam_duo`-like module on the server side. After your key authenticates, the
server prompts for a second factor.

This is a feature, not a bug, but it can be friction-heavy if you are not prepared:

- Every new SSH connection prompts for 2FA. A 96-task `rsync` opening dozens of
  parallel SSH children prompts dozens of times.
- Long-running scripts that re-SSH periodically (e.g., a checker that runs `squeue`
  every minute) will pile up Duo pushes.
- IDE-based remote tools (VS Code, Cursor) sometimes open many connections during
  startup.

The single most important mitigation is **connection multiplexing** (`ControlMaster`,
above). With multiplexing on, you authenticate once — including the 2FA push — and
every subsequent session reuses the master without re-prompting.

You generally **cannot** automate around 2FA, by design. The point of the second
factor is that it is something you *have* (your phone, a hardware token), and a
script cannot have it. Patterns that try to script around 2FA — bots that
auto-approve Duo pushes, scripts that pre-fill TOTP codes — defeat the purpose and
violate every institution's acceptable-use policy. The right answer is:

1. Enable `ControlMaster` so each interactive session prompts at most once.
2. For unattended workloads, use the cluster's actual scheduler (`sbatch`) rather
   than long-running SSH sessions from your laptop.
3. For `rsync` and `scp`, the multiplexed master makes sub-second restarts free.
4. For genuinely unattended pipelines that need to run for days, deploy them as a
   workflow manager job *on* the cluster (Snakemake or Nextflow inside a Slurm
   submission), not as something your laptop drives.

## Common Errors and What They Actually Mean

The error messages SSH produces are accurate but cryptic. The table below maps the
most common ones to their actual root cause and a one-line fix.

| Error | What it really means | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `Permission denied (publickey).` | Server has no record of your public key for this account, or the key you offered does not match anything in `authorized_keys`. | Confirm `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on the server contains your public key. Check the `User` in your config matches the remote account. Run `ssh -v` to see which key was offered. |
| `Permissions 0644 for '/Users/.../id_ed25519' are too open.` | Your private key file is world-readable. SSH refuses to use it. | `chmod 600 ~/.ssh/id_ed25519`. Also check `~/.ssh` is `700`. |
| `Host key verification failed.` | The server's host key is not in `~/.ssh/known_hosts`, or it does not match what is recorded there. | First-time connections: confirm the fingerprint with the cluster docs and accept it. If you know the cluster was rebuilt, remove the old entry: `ssh-keygen -R hpc.cluster.edu`. |
| `WARNING: REMOTE HOST IDENTIFICATION HAS CHANGED!` | Same as above, but louder. The server is presenting a different host key from the one you trusted before. | If your sysadmin announced a rebuild, this is expected — remove the stale entry and reconnect. If they did not, **stop and verify out of band** before trusting the new key. This is exactly what host-key checking is for. |
| `Connection closed by ... port 22` (immediately, with no auth attempt) | Your IP is being blocked at the firewall, or `sshd` is not running, or you are connecting to the wrong host/port. | Try from a different network. Check the cluster's status page. Confirm the hostname and port. |
| `Connection timed out` | TCP can't reach port 22 at all. Outbound 22 may be blocked by the network you are on (some hotels, conference WiFi, locked-down corporate guest networks). | Try from a phone hotspot to confirm. If 22 is blocked, ask whether the cluster offers an alternate port (often 2022 or 443). |
| `Could not resolve hostname` | DNS lookup failed for the host you typed. | Typo in `HostName`, no DNS resolution, or VPN required to resolve internal hostnames. |
| `Too many authentication failures` | The agent is offering many keys; the server's `MaxAuthTries` (often 6) trips before the right key is tried. | Add `IdentitiesOnly yes` and an explicit `IdentityFile` to the affected `Host` block so only one key is offered. |
| `kex_exchange_identification: Connection closed by remote host` | Server-side `sshd` rejected the connection before key exchange — often because of fail2ban / DenyHosts entries from too many failed logins. | Wait out the cooldown; ask the sysadmin to remove your IP from the deny list; fix whatever was causing failures (usually a bad config). |
| `ControlSocket /...is already in use` (when starting a new session) | Stale multiplexing socket from a previous SSH process. | `ssh -O exit hpc`, or just `rm ~/.ssh/cm/*` and reconnect. |
| VS Code: *Failed to connect to the remote extension host server* | Usually one of: the cluster's home quota is full so VS Code can't write to `~/.vscode-server`, the login shell prints something to stdout that confuses the launcher, or a different VS Code server version is conflicting. | Check `quota`. Ensure your `~/.bashrc` does not unconditionally `echo` anything. Delete `~/.vscode-server` and reconnect to force a clean re-install. |

When in doubt, run with verbose mode:

```bash
ssh -v hpc       # one -v: useful
ssh -vv hpc      # more
ssh -vvv hpc     # the full firehose; shows every key tried, every config rule applied
```

The verbose output names exactly which key files were considered, which were
offered, and how the server responded. For a stubborn `Permission denied (publickey)`,
`ssh -v` is almost always the fastest path to the answer.

## Best Practices

A short list of habits that prevent most SSH troubles:

- **One key per device, not one per cluster.** A single Ed25519 key per laptop,
  installed in every `authorized_keys` you use, is easier to manage and easier to
  revoke than a tangle of per-cluster keys.
- **Always set a passphrase.** A passwordless key is a plain-text password on your
  disk. Use the agent + Keychain integration so you only type the passphrase once
  per session.
- **Never commit a private key to a repository.** Add `id_*`, `*.pem`, `*.key`, and
  `id_ed25519` to a global `~/.gitignore`. If you ever do commit a private key,
  rotate it everywhere immediately — `git filter-repo` does not undo the
  fundamental fact that the key is now public.
- **Never share private keys between users or copy them between devices.** "Sharing
  a key" is sharing your identity. If a teammate needs access to a server, they
  generate their own key and you add their public key to `authorized_keys`.
- **Rotate keys when leaving an institution.** When your university affiliation
  ends, generate new keys and have your old public key removed from any
  `authorized_keys` files where it lives. Most institutional accounts are deleted
  on departure, which handles this implicitly, but cross-institution access does
  not.
- **Use `~/.ssh/config` instead of long command lines.** Every option, key path,
  jump host, and tunnel belongs in config, not in your shell history.
- **Verify host keys on first connect.** When SSH asks "are you sure you want to
  continue connecting?", check the fingerprint against your institution's
  documentation. After that initial check, host-key verification protects you
  automatically.
- **Default `ForwardAgent` to `no`.** Enable it only on hosts you trust, and prefer
  per-project deploy keys for things like Git pulls on the cluster.
- **Audit `authorized_keys` periodically.** Once a year, list the keys installed on
  your cluster account: `cat ~/.ssh/authorized_keys`. Remove any whose comment or
  fingerprint you no longer recognize. Old laptop, old key, no longer needed —
  delete the line.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pro-Tip: Treat `~/.ssh/config` Like Code

Your config grows over time as you join projects, leave institutions, and adopt
new clusters. Treat it like a real artifact: comment liberally, group related
hosts, and consider committing a sanitized copy to a personal dotfiles repository
(with private hostnames and usernames replaced or kept in an untracked
`config.local` file pulled in via `Include config.local`). When you set up a new
laptop, restoring a working SSH config is the difference between five hours of
trial-and-error and five minutes of `git clone`.
:::

## Exercises

1. Generate an Ed25519 key pair on your laptop with a meaningful comment string. Verify
   the file permissions are `600` for the private key and `644` for the public key.
   Inspect the public key file with `cat` — identify the three space-separated fields.

2. Install your public key on an HPC cluster using `ssh-copy-id`. Reconnect and confirm
   you are no longer prompted for a password. Then look at the remote `~/.ssh/
   authorized_keys` and confirm your key appears there exactly as it does in your
   local `id_ed25519.pub`.

3. Add a `Host hpc` block to your `~/.ssh/config` that uses `HostName`, `User`, and
   `IdentityFile`. Verify that `ssh hpc` connects you without any other arguments.
   Then add `ControlMaster auto`, `ControlPath`, and `ControlPersist`, and time the
   difference between the first and second connection with `time ssh hpc echo hi`.

4. Find an internal cluster node (a compute node, a service node, or a database server)
   that is only reachable via the login node. Configure a `ProxyJump` entry so you can
   reach it directly with one command. Verify by connecting and running `hostname`
   on the interior node.

5. Use `rsync` over SSH to upload a directory of fastq files to the cluster, with
   `--partial` and `--progress`. Interrupt the transfer with Ctrl-C halfway through.
   Re-run the same `rsync` command and confirm it resumes rather than starting over.

6. Start an interactive Slurm session on a compute node, launch `jupyter lab` on
   port 8888, and tunnel it through the login node so you can open it in your laptop
   browser at `http://localhost:8888`. Confirm the kernel is running on a compute
   node by running `socket.gethostname()` from a Python cell.

7. Add `IdentitiesOnly yes` to one of your `Host` blocks. Then add `-vvv` to your next
   `ssh` invocation against that host and read the output. Identify the exact line
   where the server accepts your key.

8. Examine your remote `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` on at least one cluster. For each
   line, identify whose key it is from the comment field. Remove any keys you no
   longer recognize or no longer use, then verify you can still log in.

## Further Reading

The most authoritative references are the OpenSSH manual pages, which are unusually
well-written for system tooling. `man ssh`, `man ssh_config`, `man sshd_config`, and
`man ssh-keygen` are worth reading at least once in full — together they describe
every option mentioned in this chapter and many that were not. The OpenSSH project's
own documentation at [openssh.com/manual.html](https://www.openssh.com/manual.html)
collects the same content with cross-references.

For configuration patterns, the
[Mozilla OpenSSH guidelines](https://infosec.mozilla.org/guidelines/openssh) are a
short, opinionated reference for both `ssh_config` and `sshd_config` that has aged
well. Most institutional security teams base their SSH policy on something close to
this document.

For the cryptography background, *Cryptography Engineering* by Ferguson, Schneier,
and Kohno (Wiley, 2010) remains the most readable introduction to public-key systems
that does not require a math degree. For Ed25519 specifically, Daniel J. Bernstein's
[Curve25519 page](https://cr.yp.to/ecdh.html) and the
[original Ed25519 paper](https://ed25519.cr.yp.to/) are concise and authoritative.

For HPC-specific tooling, your institution's HPC user guide is the single best source
of cluster-specific information: hostnames, ports, 2FA requirements, jump-host
topology, and any local quirks (alternate ports, IP allowlisting, VPN requirements).
For Tufts users, [it.tufts.edu/research-technology](https://it.tufts.edu/research-technology)
documents the Pax cluster's SSH configuration. Most other US universities publish
similar guides; bookmark yours.

For VS Code Remote-SSH, the
[official documentation](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/remote/ssh) covers the
basics and the
[remote-ssh troubleshooting page](https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/remote/troubleshooting)
covers the home-quota and shell-output pitfalls noted above. The Cursor documentation
mirrors this content for the Cursor fork.

For a deeper dive into the protocol itself, RFC 4251 ("The Secure Shell (SSH)
Protocol Architecture") and its companion RFCs (4252–4254) define the wire format and
authentication methods. They are dry but precise — useful when an error message
references a specific protocol concept and you want to understand exactly what is
happening on the wire.
